Compare

DataVision Pro vs Excel

Updated 2026-04-15

Excel is the world's most beloved analytics tool — and at large file sizes, repeated cleaning, or when you need real ML, it starts to creak. DataVision Pro picks up where Excel slows down: ingest large CSVs, run automated cleaning, generate predictions, and explain results with AI — without writing a single VBA macro.

Best for DataVision Pro

Analysts pushing past Excel's row limits, or anyone who reruns the same cleaning pipeline every month.

Best for Excel

Quick calculations, simple lookups, financial modelling, and anything under ~100k rows.

Side-by-side comparison

FeatureDataVision ProExcel
Max practical rows Up to 1,000,000 (Tier 3) 1,048,576 hard cap; degrades well before that
File size Up to 200 MB (Tier 3) Practical limit ~50 MB before instability
Repeatable cleaning Saved cleaning recipes Manual or VBA / Power Query
Built-in ML K-Means, RandomForest, linear models None natively (Analysis ToolPak is descriptive only)
AI chat over data Built-in Copilot in Excel 365 (separate licence) [verify]
Time-period comparison Built-in dataset history Manual workbook compare
Collaboration Web link, role-based access Excellent via Microsoft 365 / Sheets
Audit trail Step history per dataset Cell history limited; tracked changes patchy

When to choose DataVision Pro

  • Your file no longer opens cleanly in Excel.
  • You repeat the same cleaning every week / month.
  • You want AI to write the analysis paragraph for you.
  • You need clustering or prediction without leaving the analysis tool.

When to choose Excel

  • Your file is small (<50 MB) and one-off.
  • You need pivot tables, VLOOKUP, or financial functions interactively.
  • Your team's entire workflow is built on Excel and Microsoft 365.
  • You need cell-level formulas more than statistical analysis.

Frequently asked questions

Can DataVision Pro export back to Excel?

Yes — analysed datasets and reports export to CSV and Excel formats.

Does it replace pivot tables?

It replaces the analysis use of pivot tables (group-by, summarise, chart). For interactive cell-level pivoting, Excel is still excellent.

What about Google Sheets?

Same trade-offs apply. Sheets struggles past ~50k rows; DataVision Pro is comfortable into the millions.

Do I lose my Excel skills?

Not at all — the cleaning steps map 1:1 to operations you already know (filter, replace, drop column, dedupe). You just stop maintaining macros to run them.

Notes marked [verify] are pending confirmation against the latest official pricing or feature pages from Excel. We never publish a number we can't source.
